Notes: Total: 160,622 MT CO2e (2017 baseline). Watsonville pop ~52,825 (2017 est). Breakdown: Transportation 53%, Natural Gas (buildings) 28%, Electricity 11%, Solid Waste 8%. State target: 113,658 MT CO2e by 2030 (EO B-55-18). Aspirational goal: net-negative by 2030. 21.7% GHG reduction achieved since baseline (per 2018 progress report). 100% carbon-free electricity since 2018 via Monterey Bay Community Power/3CE.
Source: City of Watsonville CAAP 2030 Executive Summary

Geographic Scale: City of Watsonville (Santa Cruz County monitoring)
Notes: No city-specific monitoring station; uses county-level data. County 2019 average: 6.5 ug/m3. Watsonville is inland and adjacent to agricultural operations, so PM2.5 may be slightly higher than coastal Santa Cruz. Meets US EPA standard (12 ug/m3). Agricultural dust and fumigant use may contribute locally.
Source: IQAir; DataShare SCC; American Lung Association

Notes: City has 26 parks totaling 143 acres. City area: ~6.7 sq mi (~4,288 acres). Protected land percentage is low compared to county (32%). Watsonville Slough system is ecologically significant. City goals include protecting sloughs and natural open spaces.

Indicator: Nitrogen leached to groundwater per year
Notes: Watsonville sits in the heart of the Pajaro Valley — the primary area of agricultural nitrate contamination. Pajaro Valley Groundwater Basin has severe nitrate issues. 87% agricultural, 5% stream runoff, 4% sewer, 4% septic. Seawater intrusion is also a critical concern. Watsonville's drinking water supply comes from Pajaro Valley groundwater — directly impacted.
Source: Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency; PVWMA Basin Management Plan

Data Point: ~1 million lbs/year (county-wide; concentrated in Pajaro Valley around Watsonville)
Notes: Unlike City of Santa Cruz, Watsonville IS the center of agricultural pesticide use. 67% fumigant gases — highest in CA. Strawberry, lettuce, and berry production are primary drivers. Environmental justice concern: farmworker and residential exposure in Watsonville. CalEnviroScreen identifies south county census tracts as high pesticide exposure areas.
Source: CA DPR 2022 Pesticide Use Report; CalEnviroScreen

Indicator: Percent of waste sent to landfill
Notes: Watsonville operates its own landfill and solid waste collection. One of first US cities to implement mixed recyclable collection. City goal: extend landfill life by increasing recycling. Solid waste = 8% of city GHG emissions (2017). SB 1383 organics collection now required. City-specific diversion rate not found in public sources.
Source: City of Watsonville Solid Waste Division
